header.title: Design System + AI Feature Assembly Pipeline
header.subtitle: Parcl · Design Systems / AI Systems

sections.heading: One System. 13 Agents. Zero Hardcoded Values.
sections.body:
I designed and built a production-grade design system for a data intelligence platform — then engineered a multi-agent AI pipeline on top of it to automatically translate Figma designs into fully compliant feature code.

The two systems are tightly coupled by design. The design system provides a complete, protected component library. The agent pipeline uses it as the only valid source of UI building blocks. No agent can invent a new token. No engineer can accidentally modify a primitive. The system enforces correctness at the infrastructure level — not by asking humans to remember the rules.

sections.heading: Part 1: A Token System Built for Downstream Consumption
sections.body:
The design system is a four-layer architecture: Design Tokens → Atoms → Primitives → Composites. Every layer consumes only the layers below it. No layer skips a level. Every visual property — color, spacing, typography, shadow, border, motion, opacity — originates from a token. Zero hardcoded values anywhere in the component library.

I authored the complete token system in Figma using the Figma Tokens Plugin, with downstream engineering consumption in mind from day one. Tokens flow through an automated pipeline to CSS, TypeScript, and Tailwind — but the source of truth lives in Figma and was owned entirely on the design side.

Semantic color composition embeds opacity directly as hex alpha values. Signal yellow maps to purple in light mode — an intentional brand decision preserved exactly across themes. Spacing tokens replace Tailwind's numeric defaults entirely. The token system is a closed contract between design and engineering.

sections.heading: Part 2: 13-Agent AI Feature Assembly Pipeline
sections.body:
Building features on this design system means selecting the right puzzle pieces and assembling them correctly. That assembly process — reading a Figma design, mapping every visual property to the correct token or component, verifying all 29 rules — is deterministic enough to automate.

The pipeline starts with a single command. An orchestrator reads the Figma design and routes the work to a set of specialized agents running in parallel — each responsible for a different layer of the translation. Before any code is written, the system checks whether an existing component already solves the problem. A dedicated builder produces the output. A validator runs all 29 rules and loops the builder back on any failure.

Runtime enforcement hooks back the whole thing up at the infrastructure level — protecting the component library from modification, linting every file write against the design rules, and logging pipeline activity for observability. The model behavior and the guardrails are two separate layers of correctness.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
sections.body:
This eliminated an entire class of design-to-code errors. Any engineer can generate correctly-structured feature code from a Figma URL without needing to know the 29-rule ruleset. Every generated feature comes with a machine-verifiable validation report — 29 rules, pass/fail, file and line citations — so design review has an objective starting point instead of a subjective one.

Zero new tokens is a success metric. If a Figma value has no matching token, it's logged as @tokenDebt for a human to address. The agent never invents a value. The component library's protected status means composites and primitives are never accidentally modified during feature development. The system makes it easier to do the right thing than the wrong thing — and that's the only kind of governance that actually works.

header.title: The flow that did over $5.6 Billion
header.subtitle: Parcl · Fintech / DeFi

sections.heading: Why $5.6+ Billion Flowed Through This UI
sections.body:
People don't trade $5.6+ billion on a DeFi platform because the buttons are pretty—they do it because the experience feels right. At Parcl, I crafted a trading component UI that transacted over 41.48M real estate square feet to date. That's $5,600,000,000.00+ in cumulative notional volume.

But here's the thing: users aren't calculators. They're humans who crave simplicity, a sense of control, and a little dopamine hit.

sections.heading: It's Not Just Volume—It's What Drives It
sections.body:
From January 2024 to April 2025, Parcl's trading UI saw distinct wallet hashes climb to 25.1K while cumulative volume hit $5.6B.

That's not an accident. People don't just want to trade—they want to feel clever doing it. We designed every interaction to reward curiosity and reduce friction, turning hesitant first-timers into loyal users.

The result? Consistent growth, billions in volume, and a UI that proves a simple truth: if you make people feel good about their choices, they'll keep coming back. Let's create something for your users that's just as irresistible.

header.title: Bridging TradFi & DeFi: The Rebrand That Simplifies Real Estate Investing
header.subtitle: Parcl · FinTech, Real Estate, DeFi

sections.heading: Redefining Real Estate Investing Through Language and Design
sections.body:
If you want to change the way people think, start by changing the words they use. As Lead Product Designer and Design System Architect at Parcl, I orchestrated a rebrand that did more than update a logo—it rewired how users perceive digital real estate. Our hypothesis was simple (and a little mischievous): swap out intimidating DeFi jargon for familiar real estate language, and watch the learning curve flatten like a well-priced plot of land.

By reframing trading fees as "Closing Costs" and spotlighting price per square foot, we didn't just make the platform relatable—we made it irresistible. The result? A measurable surge in onboarding and trading, proving that sometimes, the right metaphor is worth more than a thousand tooltips.

sections.heading: Impact: Clarity, Growth, and a New Standard for RE Investing
sections.body:
UX was simplified, because it was the same as buying a home in the real world. Clarity is the ultimate growth hack.

Re-Architected a unified multi-branded design system, reducing handoff cycles by 80% and enabling rapid iteration across teams. (Efficiency isn't just a metric; it's a competitive advantage.)

Led cross-functional collaboration with founders, PMs, and engineers to deliver a scalable, accessible platform. (Great products are built by great teams, not lone wolves.)

Reframed the home page and deal flow to focus on actionable real estate opportunities, not just search.

Prioritized user context by anchoring prices to familiar home values, making DeFi accessible to a broader audience. (If you want to democratize finance, start by demystifying it.)

sections.heading: Reimagining Real Estate Speculation for the Modern User
sections.body:
As Lead Product Designer and Design System Architect at Parcl, I led the feature vision for a new real estate prediction marketplace. Inspired by the success of platforms like Polymarket and Kalshi, I architected an experience centered on simple, yes/no prediction markets—making real estate speculation accessible, engaging, and data-driven.

My focus: simplify complexity, drive measurable growth, and create a platform that meets users where they are.

sections.heading: Expected Impact
sections.body:
Expected to increase weekly traded wallets by simplifying real estate speculation into yes/no questions, attracting a broader audience.

Anticipated growth in weekly total transactions as a result of a more accessible user funnel and engaging market formats.

Projected to reengage previous traders who initially joined for points, by offering a fresh, interactive experience.

Designed to leverage Parcl Labs' data infrastructure and API, making data-backed market resolutions transparent and boosting platform engagement.

Planned integration of automated and manual email communications to increase user touchpoints and retention.

Intended to enhance social sharing and community engagement through authentication with X (Twitter), Discord, and Telegram, supporting viral growth and feedback loops.
